issues.description: Overshoot fix landed well (term walked, oscillate/crash, K as settling level, visual wobble shown). One subtlety: overshoot in the BASIC continuous logistic dN/dt=rN(K−N)/K does NOT actually occur — it monotonically approaches K. Overshoot requires reproductive time lags (or discrete-time dynamics). A sharp student could notice the stated overshoot contradicts the very equation on the slide.
issues.suggested_fix: Add a half-clause: 'real populations overshoot K because of reproductive time lags — the simple equation smooths this out, but nature doesn't.' This makes the model-vs-reality gap honest rather than self-contradicting.

issues.description: Doubling-time units fix is good (percent vs decimal flagged twice). Minor wording: 'doubling time ≈ 70/r' uses the loose 'rule of 70' (true constant is ~69.3). Fine for AP, but a precise student may ask why it's not the e-based ln(2)/r. Not misleading, just under-explained.
issues.suggested_fix: Optional: footnote 'rule of 70 — a handy approximation' so it's clearly a shortcut, not the exact continuous-growth formula.

issues.description: Mechanism test is now front-and-center and the 'same factor can flip' caveat is excellent — classifying a novel scenario is achievable. Fire appears in BOTH the density-independent example list (right column) AND as a density-dependent case in the pause (11/12). This is intentional and pedagogically strong, but a student skimming the right column then hitting pause A could feel whiplash if the contrast isn't framed as the deliberate trap it is.
issues.suggested_fix: In the right-column fire entry add a tiny asterisk: 'fire* (*usually — but see pause)' so the deliberate reuse reads as a designed test, not an inconsistency.

overall_assessment: Round-1 fixes all landed cleanly: carrying capacity is now explicitly the leveling-off/settling level with overshoot stated AND drawn (not a hard wall); r/K is clearly a spectrum with a spectrum bar and 'don't force every organism into one bin'; the density-dependent/independent MECHANISM test ('does the per-capita effect change with N?') leads the slide and is reinforced in the pause; the pause is unambiguous, well-designed to reward mechanism over memorized bins, and the answer reveal is clean and twist-aware. No critical issues. The only residual nuance is that the basic continuous logistic equation shown does not itself produce overshoot — worth a one-clause acknowledgement to avoid an apparent contradiction, but it does not create a misconception as written. Solid, exam-aligned, and pedagogically honest.
